Post_Text: You told your newest board member she's protected. You were repeating what your broker told you. Nobody checked whether it was actually true. Here's the policy detail that decides it: [LINK]

A volunteer joined a school board to help. Years later her own savings were on the line, and the policy everyone promised her had quietly stopped covering her. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)
"The school has D&O" and "I'm personally protected no matter what" are two different sentences. Most board members never learn that. They find out in a courtroom: [LINK]

'The school has D&O' and 'every board member is personally protected' are two different statements. Here is the difference, and how we close the gap without stapling on a second policy. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)
Notes: Post from company page; consider PDF lead-capture version

Post_Text: Three coverages. One shared pot of money. When the school and the board get sued together, guess who the entity drinks ahead of. This is why a volunteer's savings end up at risk: [LINK]

The board decisions most likely to draw a lawsuit are closures, layoffs, and authorizer fights. Those happen when the school has the least money to defend anyone. The exposure and the empty bank account peak on the same day: [LINK]

A broker can miss your board's exposure two ways. Sell base D&O and ignore it, or bolt a standalone protection policy on from an outside carrier that has to reconcile by hand. Both leave your volunteers exposed at the seams: [LINK]

The cleanest board protection is a reserved limit for the individual directors, written into the school's own management liability program, on paper that already agrees with itself. No second carrier. No bolt-on. No reconciliation problem. Here's what that looks like: [LINK]

One question for whoever handles your school's insurance: If the school went insolvent and a former board member were sued personally, has anyone confirmed she has protected limit the school's own legal bills can't drain first, built into the school's own program and not stapled on from outside? If the answer is a shrug, read this: [LINK]
